Wire Selection.clue: This is the primary consideration when choosing wire insulation for an aircraft installation.
choices: Suitability for the installation environment
The color of the insulation
The cost per foot
Availability from a local supplier
Wire Selection.explanation: Environmental suitability is the driving factor. Wire insulation that cannot survive its installed temperature, fluid, or abrasion environment will fail prematurely. Color, cost, and availability are all secondary.

Wire Selection.clue: This type of wire is commonly used in high-temperature areas of an aircraft, such as near the engine.
choices: PVC-insulated consumer wire
Teflon-based insulation
Rubber-insulated household wire
Bare uninsulated copper
Wire Selection.explanation: Teflon-based insulations are the aircraft standard for high-temperature runs. PVC melts at aircraft engine-compartment temperatures. Rubber household wire is not aircraft-rated. Bare conductors cannot be used without insulation.

Wire Selection.clue: In aircraft wiring, this is what the term 'gauge' refers to.
choices: The wire's operating temperature rating
The voltage rating of the insulation
The flame-resistance category of the insulation
The physical size of the wire's conductor
Wire Selection.explanation: Wire gauge describes conductor physical size (and by extension, resistance per foot and current-carrying capacity). Temperature rating, voltage rating, and flame resistance are separate properties — also important, but not what 'gauge' refers to.

Wire Selection.clue: This is the most common type of wire insulation used for general-purpose wiring in modern general-aviation aircraft.
choices: PVC (polyvinyl chloride)
ETFE (ethylene tetrafluoroethyl
Cotton braid
Uninsulated bare copper
Wire Selection.explanation: ETFE (Tefzel) is the most common general-purpose insulation in modern GA aircraft. PVC is used in consumer and automotive wiring but is not aircraft-standard. Cotton braid is obsolete. Bare copper is not an option for insulated wiring.

Wire Selection.clue: This is the approximate maximum continuous current for most AWG 22 aircraft wire in free-air chassis routing.
choices: 5 A
Wire Selection.explanation: AWG 22 aircraft wire in free-air chassis routing carries approximately 5 A continuous. 0.5 A is far too conservative. 25 A would require AWG 10 or larger. 50 A would require AWG 8 or larger.

Harness & Routing.clue: This is the most important consideration when routing aircraft wiring.
choices: Protecting the harness from chafing, abrasion, heat, and moving parts
Using the shortest possible cable length
Making the harness visible for easy inspection
Color-coordinating the harness with nearby structures
Harness & Routing.explanation: Physical protection is the driving consideration. Short length, visibility, and appearance are secondary to keeping the harness safe from mechanical and environmental damage. Chafing on a metal edge can destroy insulation in a few flight hours.

Harness & Routing.clue: This is the correct corrective action when a wire bundle is found chafed against a metal edge.
choices: Wrap the chafed area with electrical tape and continue flying
Inspect damaged wires, repair or replace, and correct the routing
Cut out the chafed section and splice in shorter wires
Ignore the damage if the circuit still functions
Harness & Routing.explanation: The correct action is both repair AND root-cause correction. Taping alone is temporary and will fail again. Splicing without rerouting leaves the same edge in place. Ignoring damage, even if the circuit currently works, risks progressive failure and in-flight fire.

Harness & Routing.clue: This is the best practice for routing a wire bundle near moving flight controls to prevent chafing.
choices: Let the bundle hang loosely so it can move out of the way
Provide adequate clearance through the full range of control motion
Tape the bundle directly to the moving control surface
Coil excess length near the control for flexibility
Harness & Routing.explanation: Clearance through full control travel plus proper securing and protective sleeving is the accepted practice. Loose bundles are not acceptable — they will move into contact with the control. Taping the bundle to a moving surface would cause immediate damage. Coiled excess creates its own chafing and weight-distribution problems.

Harness & Routing.clue: This is the primary purpose of a service loop in an aircraft wire harness.
choices: To provide extra wire length at terminations so future repairs can
To reduce the total weight of the harness
To improve signal strength through a longer conductor
To serve as an antenna for RF signals
Harness & Routing.explanation: A service loop provides reusable length for future terminations. It does not reduce weight (it adds a little), does not improve signal strength, and is not designed as an antenna.

Harness & Routing.clue: This is an acceptable method for securing wire harnesses in aircraft.
choices: Consumer-grade nylon zip ties from a hardware store
Approved aircraft cable clamps with cushion inserts
Duct tape wrapped around the harness at each support point
Steel staples driven into the airframe structure
Harness & Routing.explanation: Approved clamps at specified intervals are the correct method. Consumer-grade zip ties can fail under vibration, temperature extremes, or fluid exposure. Duct tape leaves residue, ages poorly, and is not airworthy. Staples would damage both the harness and the structure.

Connectors & Backshells.clue: This is the primary reason for using strain relief in aircraft wiring.
choices: To improve the electrical resistance of the connection
To prevent tension and bending loads from damaging the termination
To reduce the overall weight of the harness
To provide a path for draining moisture
Connectors & Backshells.explanation: Strain relief transfers mechanical load away from the termination point. It does not affect electrical resistance. It adds a small amount of weight. It is not a drainage feature.

Connectors & Backshells.clue: This is the primary function of a connector in an aircraft wiring system.
choices: To provide a mating interface for disconnection and reconnection
To increase the voltage traveling through the harness
To convert AC to DC
To act as a circuit breaker during faults
Connectors & Backshells.explanation: Connectors provide a maintainable mating interface. They do not change voltage, convert AC to DC, or act as circuit protection — those are separate components.

Connectors & Backshells.clue: This is the primary purpose of a backshell on an aircraft connector.
choices: To serve as a backup electrical ground path
To display the connector's part number for identification
To increase the connector's contact density
To provide cable strain relief, environmental sealing, shield
Connectors & Backshells.explanation: Backshells are primarily mechanical and EMI features: strain relief, environmental sealing, shield termination, and cable exit-angle control. They are not identification markers, do not increase contact count, and while they may provide an EMI ground they are not an electrical backup ground.

Connectors & Backshells.clue: This tool is commonly used to install connector pins on aircraft wiring.
choices: A consumer-grade adjustable crimper from a hardware store
Ordinary pliers, squeezed firmly
A soldering iron and solder
An approved aircraft crimp tool with a positioner matched to the pin type
Connectors & Backshells.explanation: An approved aircraft crimp tool with the correct positioner for each pin type is the standard. Consumer crimpers produce inconsistent crimps. Pliers crush rather than form the barrel correctly. Soldering is used for some connectors but the majority of modern aircraft pins are crimp-terminated.

Connectors & Backshells.clue: This is the correct response to a coworker who suggests using automotive wire connectors on an avionics rack.
choices: Automotive connectors lack the qualification and traceability required for aircraft avionics
Agree, because automotive connectors are cheaper and readily available
Suggest using them only for non-critical signals
Use them temporarily and replace them at the next inspection
Connectors & Backshells.explanation: Automotive connectors are not approved for aircraft avionics installations under any circumstance, including 'non-critical' signals. Temporary use followed by later replacement still violates the installation requirements and creates legal, safety, and documentation problems.

Crimping & Soldering.clue: This is the type of solder typically used in avionics work.
choices: Acid-core plumbing solder
Tin/lead rosin-core solder
Silver-bearing brazing rod
Aluminum-brazing flux paste
Crimping & Soldering.explanation: Tin/lead rosin-core solder (Sn63/Pb37) is the avionics standard. Acid-core solder is strictly for plumbing and would corrode electrical connections. Silver brazing and aluminum brazing are used in metal fabrication, not avionics wiring.

Crimping & Soldering.clue: This is a critical step for making a good solder joint.
choices: Applying solder to the iron tip first, then touching the iron to a cold joint
Using as much solder as possible to ensure complete coverage
Avoiding flux, because it contaminates the joint
Heating the parent metal (wire and terminal) to the proper temperature
Crimping & Soldering.explanation: The critical step is heating the parent metal first so the solder flows into the joint. Touching the iron to a cold joint produces a cold solder joint with poor electrical and mechanical properties. Excess solder does not improve the joint. Rosin flux is required — it cleans the metal so solder can wet the surface.

Crimping & Soldering.clue: This type of splice is generally preferred for environmentally sensitive areas of an aircraft such as near the engine or in a wheel well.
choices: Twist-and-tape splice
Environmentally sealed crimp splice with heat-shrink adhesive
Wire-nut connector
Open butt splice with no covering
Crimping & Soldering.explanation: Sealed heat-shrink crimp splices are the aircraft standard for environmentally sensitive areas. Twist-and-tape, wire-nuts, and open splices admit moisture, fuel, and oil, leading to corrosion and intermittent connections. Wire-nuts are not acceptable on aircraft at all.

Crimping & Soldering.clue: This is the correct action when a coax cable's center conductor is found nicked.
choices: Apply a dab of solder to rebuild the nicked area and continue
Remove the damaged section and replace it with a new cable length or properly
Tape over the nick with electrical tape
Ignore the nick if the signal still passes
Crimping & Soldering.explanation: A nicked center conductor alters coax impedance and causes signal reflections. The proper fix is replacement of the affected section. Soldering over a nick does not restore uniform impedance. Tape does not repair the conductor itself. Ignoring a known defect is unacceptable.

Crimping & Soldering.clue: This is the typical acceptable twist rate for aircraft safety wire installed with the double-twist method using 0.032-inch wire.
choices: 1 to 2 twists per inch
15 to 20 twists per inch
30 to 40 twists per inch
6 to 8 twists per inch
Crimping & Soldering.explanation: The typical specification is 6 to 8 twists per inch for 0.032-inch safety wire, double-twist method. Values of 1-2 would be too loose to secure against vibration; 15-20 or higher would overwork the wire and cause breakage.

EMI & Shielding.clue: This is the primary reason shielded wire is used in avionics systems.
choices: To increase the wire's current-carrying capacity
To improve mechanical strength of the cable
To provide a backup ground path for power circuits
To reduce electromagnetic interference (EMI)
EMI & Shielding.explanation: Shielded wire exists primarily to control EMI. It does not meaningfully change current capacity (that is determined by the conductor gauge). It adds some mechanical mass but is not used for mechanical strength. It is not a backup power ground — the shield is typically a signal-ground reference, not a power return.

EMI & Shielding.clue: This is a commonly followed guideline for shield termination to reduce EMI in avionics wiring.
choices: Leave the shield unterminated and floating at both ends
Terminate the shield with a 360-degree connection to the backshell or chassis
Connect the shield only through a long pigtail wire to the nearest screw
Twist the shield together with a signal conductor and route both to the same pin
EMI & Shielding.explanation: 360-degree shield termination to the backshell or chassis provides uniform EMI coverage. Pigtails leave gaps in the electromagnetic envelope and create a series inductance that degrades high-frequency shielding. A floating shield provides essentially no EMI benefit. Connecting shield to a signal pin would short the shield to the signal — a major error.

EMI & Shielding.clue: This instrument is best suited to measuring very low resistance in an aircraft bonding system.
choices: A milliohmmeter (bonding meter) using four-wire Kelvin
A standard handheld DMM on the ohms setting
A megohmmeter (megger) at 500 V
A clamp ammeter measuring AC current
EMI & Shielding.explanation: A milliohmmeter is purpose-built for low-resistance measurement using four-wire Kelvin technique. A standard DMM lacks the resolution and is dominated by lead resistance. A megohmmeter is for insulation resistance at the opposite end of the range. A clamp ammeter measures current, not resistance.

EMI & Shielding.clue: This is the BEST next step when a milliohmmeter reading fluctuates significantly while testing a bonding strap.
choices: Remove the bond hardware, clean the mating surfaces to bare metal, reinstall
Accept the reading and sign off the bond as good
Ignore the fluctuation and use the lowest observed reading
Replace the milliohmmeter because its calibration is drifting
EMI & Shielding.explanation: Fluctuating readings indicate an intermittent physical connection, almost always at one of the terminations. Remove, clean, retorque, retest. Accepting or averaging the reading leaves a bad bond in service. Replacing the meter without evidence of its failure is premature.

EMI & Shielding.clue: This is what shear wire is designed to do.
choices: Break deliberately at a defined force to indicate that a component has been actuated
Carry electrical current as a primary conductor
Support the weight of engine mounts
Provide a bonding path between airframe structures
EMI & Shielding.explanation: Shear wire's purpose is deliberate mechanical failure at a calibrated force, to indicate actuation. It is not an electrical conductor, not a structural support, and not a bonding component.

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This is the FIRST step a technician takes when a wire bundle is found chafed against a metal edge during inspection.
choices: Wrap the chafed area with tape and return the aircraft to service
Ignore the finding as long as the circuit still works
Inspect the damaged wires, repair or replace them, then correct the routing or install edge protection to prevent recurrence
Replace the entire harness as a precaution
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: A chafed bundle demands both repair and root-cause correction. The first step is to assess damage and address the cause — rerouting or adding edge protection. Tape is a temporary expedient; ignoring damage is prohibited; full replacement is excessive for a localized problem.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Scenario: During an aircraft inspection, you find that a wire bundle is damaged due to rubbing against a metal edge. What should be your first step in addressing this issue?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This is the approximate maximum continuous current rating for AWG 22 wire in a typical bundled aircraft installation.
choices: About 5 amperes
About 20 amperes
About 50 amperes
About 100 amperes
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Per AC 43.13-1B wiring tables, AWG 22 carries roughly 5 A continuous in typical bundled aircraft installations (somewhat higher in free-air/chassis applications). 20 A, 50 A, and 100 A ratings belong to much heavier gauges.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: What is the maximum allowable current for most aircraft wiring systems using AWG 22 wire?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This is the correct action when a newly installed wire bundle will not fit its designated routing path.
choices: Force the bundle into place with hand tools
Cut a larger passage through airframe structure
Stop, consult the installation drawing and AMM, and select an approved alternate path or request engineering disposition
Shave insulation off individual wires to reduce bundle diameter
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Approved documentation is the controlling authority. If the designated path cannot be used, the technician stops, reviews the drawings, and seeks an approved alternate — or engineering guidance. Forcing, structural modification, and insulation removal are never acceptable responses.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Scenario: During an avionics installation, you notice that the wire bundle does not fit the designated routing path. What should you do next?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This is the most likely circuit topology when one lamp in a three-lamp navigation circuit fails but the other two keep operating.
choices: Series
Parallel
Shunt-only
Open loop
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Parallel wiring keeps each lamp on its own branch, so losing one does not break the others. A series circuit would extinguish all three when any lamp opens. 'Open loop' and 'shunt-only' are not standard lighting topologies.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: In a navigation light circuit, if one bulb fails but the other two remain lit, the lights are most likely connected in:

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This routing practice best reduces electromagnetic interference on a new aircraft lighting feed that runs near other electrical systems.
choices: Separate it from high-current wiring, use shielded cable where specified, and cross power paths at right angles
Bundle the new feed in the same loom as primary power for neatness
Run it parallel to the main DC bus for its full length
Leave it unsupported between end points to let it move freely
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Separation from power wiring, use of specified shielding, and right-angle crossings minimize inductive and capacitive coupling. Common-bundling and parallel runs maximize interference; loose wire creates mechanical damage rather than EMI control.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are wiring a new aircraft lighting system. The wiring must be routed in a way that avoids electromagnetic interference from other electrical systems. What should you do to minimize interference?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This describes how a wire harness is typically routed and supported in a small GA aircraft environment.
choices: Unsupported between both ends
Clamped or tied at intervals of 24 inches or less, clear of moving parts and sharp edges, and protected where chafing is possible
All circuits combined into a single airframe-length loom
Secured directly to a moving flight control to keep it out of the way
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: AC 43.13-1B sets the 24-inch maximum support spacing and requires clearance from sharp edges and moving parts, plus protection at any possible chafe point. Unsupported runs, single-loom schemes, and attachment to moving surfaces all violate good practice.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: How should a wire harness generally be routed and secured in a small GA aircraft environment?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This is the PRIMARY function provided by a backshell on an aircraft connector.
choices: Mechanical strain relief, cable support, and shield termination on EMI versions
Higher pin current rating
A substitute for the connector's coupling mechanism
Color identification of the bundle
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: A backshell strain-relieves and supports the cable at the rear of the connector, and on EMI versions, terminates the cable shield to the shell. It does not change pin current rating or replace latching hardware.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Backshells are used on aircraft connectors PRIMARILY to provide:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This is the tool a technician should use to make a secure and repeatable termination when replacing a crimped wire connector.
choices: A calibrated M22520-series crimping tool with the correct positioner for the contact
A propane torch with solder
A general-purpose multi-tool
Slip-joint pliers
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Aircraft crimp terminations are specified by controlled crimp force and tool cycle. A calibrated M22520 (or manufacturer equivalent) with the correct positioner is the only approved tool. Pliers, torches, and general-purpose multi-tools cannot meet the specification.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are performing maintenance on an aircraft and need to replace a wire connector. Which tool should you use to ensure a secure and reliable connection?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This installation practice must be avoided when performing aircraft wiring work.
choices: Using a calibrated crimping tool on each contact
Supporting the harness every 24 inches with approved clamps
Substituting automotive-grade connectors for aviation-approved types
Applying heat-shrink solder sleeves in environmentally exposed areas
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Automotive connectors are not qualified for aircraft vibration, temperature, or humidity. The other choices are all required or recommended aviation practices under AC 43.13-1B.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: When performing an aircraft wiring installation, which practice should be avoided?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This is the most appropriate method for terminating wires in an aircraft installation.
choices: Crimp to aviation-approved contacts with a calibrated tool, or solder and finish with a heat-shrink sleeve where specified
Use a residential wire nut secured with tape
Twist and tape the ends by hand
Solder the wires directly with no strain relief
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Calibrated crimping with approved contacts is the aviation standard; soldering is used where specified, always finished with a heat-shrink sleeve for strain relief. Twist-and-tape, wire nuts, and unsupported solder terminations are prohibited.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Which method is most appropriate for terminating aircraft wiring?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This is the step that matters most when selecting wire gauge for a new avionics installation.
choices: Matching the gauge used elsewhere on the aircraft without calculation
Copying the gauge used on a different aircraft model
Selecting the thinnest wire that fits the connector pins
Calculating the gauge from the equipment's current draw, acceptable voltage drop, and operating environment
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Gauge is a calculated value based on current, voltage drop, and environment. AC 43.13-1B tables and the installation manual provide the data. Copying unrelated circuits or fitting to the connector can produce overheating or excessive voltage drop.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Which step is critical for selecting the correct wire gauge during a new avionics equipment installation?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: Handling Teflon-insulated avionics wire with automatic strippers, this is the recommended practice.
choices: Use a hot-blade (thermal) stripper to melt through the insulation
Use a precision controlled-depth mechanical stripper with the correct blade for the gauge
Score the insulation with a knife and pull it off by hand
Use a general-purpose stripper at its widest setting
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: Precision mechanical stripping with the correct blade cleanly removes Teflon without nicking the conductor. Thermal strippers can damage Teflon and release fumes; oversize or knife methods risk conductor nicks that fail in service.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: Which practice is recommended when using automatic wire strippers on Teflon-coated wires for avionics installations?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: This is the FIRST check when a freshly installed avionics system will not power up.
choices: Firmware version in the unit's memory
Condition of the exterior paint around the antenna
GPS satellite acquisition status
Wiring — check for shorts, verify correct power and ground, and inspect pin seating
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: New-install power-up failures are overwhelmingly wiring problems — shorts, reversed power, or unseated contacts. Wiring verification comes first and protects the equipment. Firmware, GPS, and paint are not initial diagnostics.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are tasked with wiring a new avionics system into an aircraft. After completing the installation, the system fails to power up. Which of the following is the FIRST thing you should check?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: This is the BEST corrective action when a harness is discovered with a damaged outer jacket.
choices: Inspect underlying conductors and then repair the jacket with approved sleeving/heat-shrink, or replace the affected section if conductors are also damaged
Wrap the area with standard electrical tape and release the aircraft
Ignore the damage if the circuit is still operating
Remove and replace the entire harness even if damage is local
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: Condition-based repair is the standard: verify conductor condition, then repair the jacket with approved protection or replace the affected section. Tape is not a permanent repair; ignoring damage is prohibited; full replacement is excessive when localized repair is allowed.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: During inspection, you find a harness with a damaged outer jacket. Which of the following is the b Best corrective action?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: This occurs when a shielded wire is installed with the shield left floating at both ends.
choices: The shield provides maximum EMI protection because it is isolated
The shield's signal current-carrying capacity improves
The shield grounds itself automatically through capacitance to the airframe
The ungrounded shield behaves as an antenna and can increase EMI coupling instead of reducing it
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: A shield with no ground path cannot dump induced currents and acts as an antenna. The proper fix is to ground the shield per the installation manual (single-point or both-ends as specified). Capacitive coupling is not a compliant ground, and shields do not carry signal current.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: If you discover a shielded wire with the shield floating at both ends, what occurs?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: Building a harness from a wiring diagram, this is typically the opening step of the process.
choices: Cut every wire to the estimated length and bundle them immediately
Install the unmade harness into the aircraft and mark break-outs in place
Crimp connectors to both ends of every wire before routing
Lay out the diagram and plan the harness — identifying each run, connector location, and break-out length — before cutting
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: Harness fabrication begins with layout on a jig or board from the wiring diagram. Cutting, crimping, and installation come after the plan is verified. Skipping layout produces length errors and rework.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: When fabricating a wire harness from a wiring diagram, what is the FIRST step you should typically take?

final.clue: To prevent electromagnetic interference (EMI), AC power lines and sensitive sensor wiring (like ARINC databuses) must be separated by what absolute minimum clearance if they must run parallel without physical barriers?
choices: 0.5 inches
2.0 inches
6.0 inches
They cannot run parallel under any circumstances
final.explanation: Standard wiring practices (MIL-W-5088L and AC 43.13-1B) dictate a minimum 2-inch clearance between power/control cables and sensitive signal/radio wiring where possible, though 6 inches is preferred.

final.clue: When using a mil-spec Daniels indent crimper with a positioner, what prevents the tool from being opened mid-crimp, guaranteeing a full compression cycle?
choices: The ratcheting pawl
The turret lock
The go/no-go gauge
The safety wire seal
final.explanation: A precision ratcheting mechanism ensures the tool handles must be fully compressed before they can be released, preventing under-crimped pins.

final.clue: When terminating a shielded wire via a shield drain loop or solder sleeve, the unshielded portion of the inner conductor should generally not exceed what length to maintain EMI protection?
choices: 0.5 to 1.0 inch
2.0 to 3.0 inches
There is no limit
Quarter wavelength of the signal
final.explanation: To maintain shielding effectiveness and prevent the exposed wire from acting like an antenna, the 'window' of unshielded wire at a termination should be kept as short as possible, typically under 1 inch.
